findings.summary: Refusal/error phrases are defined in three separate, partially overlapping locations with no single source of truth
evidence: config.py lines 113-119: default_ocr_refusal_markers defines 5 phrases used by OCR provider at runtime
classifier/constants.py lines 62-68: ERROR_PHRASES tuple defines 5 phrases (partially overlapping) used by classifier quality_gates
classifier/prompts.py lines 33-35: hardcodes 3 error-detection phrases in the LLM system prompt text

OCR provider uses settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS while classifier uses constants.ERROR_PHRASES - these lists partially overlap but diverge (e.g. config has 'i can't assist' while constants has 'i'm sorry, i can't assist with that.')
findings.suggestion: Consolidate refusal phrases into a single source (e.g. a shared constant in common/) and derive the config defaults and classifier constants from it. Reference the canonical list in the prompt template via interpolation.

findings.summary: Inconsistent exception catching: some call sites catch specific exceptions while parallel call sites use bare 'except Exception'
evidence: tags.py get_latest_tags line 79 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError) for get_document()
claims.py claim_processing_tag lines 52, 77, 87 catch bare Exception for the same get_document() and update_document_metadata() calls
stale_lock.py lines 51, 78 catch bare Exception for get_documents_by_tag() and update_document_metadata()
ocr/worker.py _refresh_document line 155 catches bare Exception for get_document()
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a consistent exception set for PaperlessClient calls (e.g. (OSError, httpx.HTTPError)) across all call sites, or define a PaperlessClient-level exception type that wraps transport errors.

findings.summary: Tag lifecycle operations silently swallow exceptions without propagation or recovery, making failures invisible to callers
evidence: tags.py remove_stale_queue_tag lines 114-121: catches bare Exception, logs, but does not propagate or return failure status
tags.py release_processing_tag lines 152-172: catches bare Exception in two places, logs, returns None regardless
stale_lock.py recover_stale_locks lines 78-83: catches bare Exception per doc, logs, continues silently
Callers have no way to know these operations failed, potentially leaving stale tags indefinitely
findings.suggestion: For release_processing_tag and remove_stale_queue_tag, return a boolean indicating success/failure so callers can take corrective action or emit metrics. At minimum, add structured fields to the log events so monitoring can alert on repeated failures.

findings.summary: finalize_document_with_error accepts a logger instance as a parameter, breaking the convention that modules use their own module-level logger
evidence: tags.py finalize_document_with_error line 184: takes 'log: structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger' as a parameter
ocr/worker.py line 275: passes its module-level log to finalize_document_with_error
classifier/worker.py line 239: passes its module-level log to finalize_document_with_error
Every other function in the codebase uses module-level 'log = structlog.get_logger(__name__)' without parameter passing
findings.suggestion: Remove the log parameter from finalize_document_with_error and use a module-level logger instead. If caller context is needed, use structlog context variables (structlog.contextvars).

findings.summary: library_setup.py mutates the openai module's global state (base_url, api_key, http_client), creating implicit coupling across all modules that use the openai SDK
evidence: library_setup.py lines 57-62: sets openai.base_url, openai.api_key, openai.http_client as module-level globals
llm.py line 35: OpenAIChatMixin._create_completion calls openai.chat.completions.create() relying on this implicit global state
No explicit client instance is passed - all LLM consumers silently depend on setup_libraries() having been called first
If setup_libraries() is not called (e.g. in tests), openai calls will use unexpected defaults or fail unpredictably
findings.suggestion: Create an explicit openai.Client instance in library_setup and pass it through Settings or a dedicated holder, so the dependency is visible and testable.

findings.summary: OCR and classifier providers track stats with the same pattern (thread lock + dict) but with different stat keys, different reset behavior, and no shared abstraction
evidence: ocr/provider.py lines 99-115: initializes _stats in __init__ with 4 keys, never resets
classifier/provider.py lines 52-68: initializes _stats via _reset_stats() method with 7 keys, resets on each classify_text call
Both use identical _inc_stat() and get_stats() method patterns with threading.Lock
OCR provider accumulates stats across all pages of a document; classifier resets per call - inconsistent lifecycle
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared ProviderStats helper class with configurable keys and optional reset, used by both providers.

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon uses string attribute names to look up tag IDs from Settings, bypassing type checking
evidence: bootstrap.py line 73: getattr(settings, processing_tag_id_attr) where processing_tag_id_attr is a string like 'OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID'
bootstrap.py line 74: getattr(settings, pre_tag_id_attr) where pre_tag_id_attr is a string like 'PRE_TAG_ID'
ocr/daemon.py lines 77-78: passes string literals 'OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID' and 'PRE_TAG_ID'
classifier/daemon.py lines 54-55: passes string literals 'CLASSIFY_PROCESSING_TAG_ID' and 'CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID'
A typo in any of these strings would only fail at runtime, not at type-check time
findings.suggestion: Accept the tag IDs directly as int|None parameters instead of string attribute names, or use a small dataclass/TypedDict for daemon-specific tag configuration.

findings.summary: Both daemons create a new PaperlessClient (and thus a new httpx.Client TCP connection pool) per document, discarding connection reuse benefits
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 44: 'paperless = PaperlessClient(settings)' inside _process_document, called for every document
classifier/daemon.py line 77: 'paperless = PaperlessClient(settings)' inside process_document closure, called for every document
Each PaperlessClient.__init__ creates a new httpx.Client (paperless.py line 38)
The list_client used for polling is a separate long-lived instance, showing the per-document pattern is a deliberate choice but wasteful
findings.suggestion: Use a shared PaperlessClient per worker thread (or a connection pool), rather than creating and destroying a client per document.

findings.summary: pyproject.toml pins httpx~=0.27 but installed version is 0.28, indicating the version constraint is stale
evidence: pyproject.toml line: 'httpx~=0.27'
pip list shows 'httpx 0.28.1' installed
The ~= compatible release operator means >=0.27, <0.28, so 0.28.1 technically violates the constraint
This works only because the package was installed in editable mode, bypassing the constraint
findings.suggestion: Update the httpx pin to ~=0.28 (or >=0.27,<1.0 if broader compatibility is intended) to match the actually running version.

findings.summary: OCR worker catches RuntimeError specifically from bytes_to_images but lets all other exceptions in the workflow propagate unhandled to the thread pool
evidence: ocr/worker.py line 96: catches RuntimeError specifically from bytes_to_images and routes to _finalize_with_error
ocr/worker.py: no top-level try/except in process() - if download_content() or _update_paperless_document() raises, the exception propagates to daemon_loop's thread pool handler
classifier/worker.py: similarly no top-level try/except in process() - if classify_text or _apply_classification raise unexpected errors, they propagate without error-tagging the document
daemon_loop.py line 55: catches and logs these as 'Work item failed' but the document is left in a claimed state without error tag
findings.suggestion: Add a top-level try/except in both workers' process() methods that catches unexpected exceptions, calls _finalize_with_error, and ensures the document is not left in an ambiguous state.

findings.summary: classifier.quality_gates imports from common.utils for is_error_content but also imports from classifier.constants for ERROR_PHRASES, blending two error-phrase sources across package boundaries
evidence: quality_gates.py line 11: 'from common.utils import is_error_content'
quality_gates.py line 12: 'from .constants import ERROR_PHRASES, GENERIC_DOCUMENT_TYPES'
is_error_content in common.utils accepts error_phrases as a parameter but has no awareness of what those phrases are
The classifier provides its own ERROR_PHRASES which partially overlap with config.py's OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS - same concept split across packages
findings.suggestion: Unify the error-phrase definitions in common/ so both OCR and classifier draw from a single authoritative list, with per-daemon overrides as needed.
